1: hodu lYHWH qiru bishmo — three Hiphil imperatives open the psalm: hodu (give thanks/acknowledge, from yadah), qiru (call upon), hodihu (make known). The triad frames the psalm's purpose: communal proclamation to the nations (baammim). Ps 105 is a historical recital of covenant faithfulness; 1 Chr 16:8 quotes vv 1-15 at the ark's installation, rooting this liturgy in Israel's worship.
2: sihu bkhol-niplootayw — sihu (reflect/meditate/speak, Qal imperative of siah) ranges from inner meditation to outspoken recitation. The object niplootayw (his wondrous acts, Niphal participle of palah) denotes acts so astonishing they exceed human capacity — the word family includes what is 'too difficult' for man but not for God (Gen 18:14).
3: hithallelu bshem qodshow — hithallelu is Hithpael of halal, the root of hallelujah: reflexive boasting in the LORD. The command targets the inward community (ybaqqshe YHWH, those who seek the LORD) rather than outsiders. Joy (yismhu) flows from seeking, not from circumstances.
4: dirshu YHWH vuzzow — darash (seek/inquire) and baqqesh (seek/search) are near-synonyms deployed in parallel, intensifying the call. uzzow (his strength/power) stands in apposition to phanayw (his face/presence) — to seek God's face is to seek his active power, not merely information about him. bkhol-et (at all times) removes seasonal limits on the pursuit.
5: zikru niplootayw asher asah — the Qal imperative zikru (remember) is not passive nostalgia but active liturgical recall. Three objects follow: niplootayw (wonders), mophetayw (signs/portents), and mishpte-phihu (judgments of his mouth). The third term is striking — God's spoken verdicts are as much his deeds as the physical plagues.
6: zera avraham avdow — the addressees are identified through two covenant lineages: zera avraham (seed of Abraham, covenant-descent language from Gen 12-17) and bne yaaqov bchirayw (sons of Jacob, his chosen ones). bachar (election) is the key verb of this psalm: God does not merely acknowledge Israel but actively selects them. eved (servant) for Abraham is an honorific of intimate covenantal relationship.
7: hu YHWH elohenu — the emphatic pronoun hu (he, himself) asserts exclusive identity: this same LORD is our God. mishpatayw bkhol-haaretz (his judgments/justice throughout all the earth) — what might seem a private national covenant carries universal jurisdiction. The combination of particular covenant and universal sovereignty is characteristic of Israel's theology.
8: zachar briytow leolam — zachar (remember) used of God is covenantal action, not mere cognition: his remembering activates faithfulness (cf. Gen 8:1, Exod 2:24). dabar tziwwah leelep dor (the word he commanded to a thousand generations) — thousand is not a precise count but an idiom of incalculable duration, echoing the fifth commandment's promise (Deut 5:10).
9: asher karat et-avraham — karat brit (cut a covenant) preserves the ancient rite of covenant ratification through animal division (Gen 15:9-18). The oath sworn to Isaac (nishba lyitzchaq) adds a second generational anchor, showing the covenant is not a one-time event but a promise renewed across the patriarchal line.
10: wayyaamideha lyaaqov lchok — heeemid (caused to stand, Hiphil of amad) conveys establishment, durability. chok (statute/binding decree, from chaqaq, to inscribe) describes the covenant as a carved-in obligation — not a revocable policy. brit olam (everlasting covenant) for Israel echoes the same phrase used for Noah (Gen 9:16) and Abraham (Gen 17:7, 13, 19).
11: lcha ettten et-eretz knaaan — the direct speech of God embedded in historical recital. chevel nachalatckem — chevel (rope/measuring line, then the portion measured by it) is the idiom for the allotted territorial inheritance. The surveyor's rope (chevel) divides tribal portions (cf. Josh 17:14; Mic 2:4-5); to receive a chevel is to receive a deeded inheritance.
12: bheyotam mte mispar — mte mispar (men of number) is an idiom for a small, countable group — few enough that you could number them (cf. Deut 4:27; Jer 44:28). kmat wgarim bah (almost few and sojourners in it) — ger (sojourner) had no legal land rights, making God's gift of the land all the more striking.
13: wayyithhallkhu migoy el-goy — hithhallek (Hithpael of halakh) describes repeated, ongoing movement — the patriarchs were perpetually mobile, never settled. The repetition of the preposition (migoy...el-goy, mimmamlacha...el-am) underscores the precariousness of their situation as stateless wanderers among empires.
14: lo-hinniach adam leoshqam — hinniach (Hiphil of nuach) means to permit or allow; God did not allow any man to oppress them. wayyochach alaychem mlakhim — yakhah (Hiphil, to rebuke/reprove) used of God correcting kings on behalf of the powerless patriarchs: Pharaoh (Gen 12:17), Abimelech (Gen 20:3-7). Kings are answerable to the covenant God.
15: al-tigeu bimshichai — mashiach (anointed) applied to the patriarchs is exceptional — they hold no formal office, yet God treats them as his consecrated ones. nbiyay (my prophets) identifies Abraham especially (Gen 20:7: 'he is a prophet'). The warning protects those who carry the promise; to harm them is to assault the covenant itself.
16: wayyiqra raav al-haaretz — the verb qara (call/summon) applied to the famine is theologically charged: the famine is not a natural disaster but a divine summons. kol-matteh-lechem shabar — matteh-lechem (staff of bread) is the idiom for the food supply (Lev 26:26; Ezek 4:16; 5:16): God breaks the very structural support of nutrition, channeling all provision through Joseph.
17: shalach liphneyhem ish — yosef — the syntax is deliberate: shalach (he sent) appears before Joseph's name, reframing the sale by his brothers as divine mission. leved nimkar (who was sold as a slave) — the passive voice (nimkar, Niphal of makar) distances the psalmist from fraternal guilt while naming the historical fact. Joseph's slavery is the vehicle of covenant advance.
18: annu bakevel raglow — anah (Piel, to afflict/humble) is the intensive form used for Egypt's later affliction of Israel (Exod 1:12). barzel baah naphshow (iron entered his soul) — a vivid idiom: the iron fetters penetrate not just the body but the person (nephesh). The image suggests that Joseph internalized the affliction — the prison was not merely external confinement but entered his whole being.
19: imrat YHWH tzrapphathu — tzaraph (to smelt/refine metals) applied to the divine word: the word of God that Joseph had received tested and purified him across years of waiting. The time of suffering (ad-et bo devaro, until the time his word came) is recast as a metallurgical process — affliction as the furnace that proves genuine faith.
20: shalach melek wayyattirehu — natar (Piel, to release/let go) and pattach (Piel, to open/set free) in parallel describe the reversal of Joseph's imprisonment. The structural echo with v17 is intentional: God sent Joseph into slavery (v17); now the king sends to release him. Both sendings are under divine governance.
21: samo adon lveytow — adon (lord/master) and mosher (ruler) bracket Joseph's authority. bkhol-qinyanow (all his possessions/acquisitions) — qinyan (from qanah, acquire) is an economic term for accumulated assets. Pharaoh's entire estate is placed under the man who had nothing — the reversal is total.
22: lesor sarayw bnaphshow — the irony is pointed: Joseph (asur, bound, v18) now binds (asar) princes at his pleasure (bnaphshow). uzqenayw yechakkem (he taught wisdom to his elders) — chakhem Piel (cause to be wise): the former prisoner educates Egypt's ruling class. The schema of humiliation-then-exaltation that recurs through Scripture is on full display.
23: wayyavo yisrael mitzrayim — yisrael here is Jacob the patriarch (the same man as in v6), making explicit that the nation's descent begins with one man's journey. eretz-cham (land of Ham) is the archaic poetic name for Egypt, tracing the Egyptians' ancestry to Noah's son Ham (Gen 10:6). The name appears only in the Psalms (78:51; 105:23, 27; 106:22).
24: wayyepher et-amow meod — parah (to be fruitful) echoes the creation mandate (Gen 1:28) and the patriarchal promises (Gen 17:20; 28:3). The language is deliberate: Israel in Egypt is fulfilling the Adamic commission even in exile. wayyaamtzehu mitzarayw (he made them stronger than their adversaries) — the very growth that alarmed Egypt was divinely engineered.
25: haphak libbam lisno amow — haphak (to turn/overturn) is the verb of Sodom and Gomorrah's destruction (Gen 19:21, 25). Here God turns hearts toward hatred. The theological claim is stark: Pharaoh's hostility is neither accidental nor autonomous but part of God's larger plan to create the crisis that will demonstrate his power. Cf. Exod 4:21; 9:12 (hardening).
26: shalach moshe avdow aharon asher bachar-bow — shalach (he sent) for the third time (Joseph v17; Moses here). The sending pattern shows the psalmist's theology: God's human agents are always sent ones. bachar-bow (whom he chose in him) — bachar (election) applied to Aaron alongside Moses; both prophet and priest are elected instruments.
27: samu-bam divrey ototayw — a difficult construction: lit. 'they set among them words of his signs.' The phrase indicates that Moses and Aaron displayed and enacted the signs, or that the signs themselves were communicative acts — words spoken in deed. mophetim beretz cham links to the historical summary in Ps 78:43-51 and the tradition's consistent use of ot + mophet as a fixed pair for Exodus miracles.
28: shalach choshech wayyachashik — the order of plagues here is not Exodus chronological: darkness (ninth plague) comes first, framing all the others. welo-maru et-devaro (and they did not rebel against his word) — the subject is Moses and Aaron, not Egypt: they remained obedient throughout. The psalmist commends the messengers alongside the miraculous acts.
29: haphak et-meyhem ldam — haphak again (vv25, 29): God who turned hearts toward hatred now turns water to blood. The repetition may be intentional — the same overturning power operates in the moral realm (hearts) and the physical realm (water). wayamet et-dghatam (killed their fish) — fish were a dietary staple in Egypt (Num 11:5), making this a fundamental economic and food security crisis.
30: sharats artzam tzphardim — sharats (to swarm, teem) echoes the creation language of Gen 1:20, where the waters swarm with living creatures. The frogs overrunning Egypt mock the creation order: proliferation that should be blessing becomes plague. chadre malchehem (the inner rooms of their kings) — the plague penetrates even the most protected spaces of power.
31: amar wayyavo arov — amar (he spoke/said) without any specific speech content: the bare divine word is enough to summon the plague. The minimalism is theological — creation and judgment both proceed by dabar alone. kinnim (gnats/lice) and arov (swarms of insects) represent the relentless multiplication of judgment.
32: natan gishmeyhem barad — natan (he gave) inverts the blessing idiom: what God gives is hail instead of rain. Rain is gift; hail is judgment wearing rain's form. esh lehavot beartzam (flashing fire in their land) — fire within hail (Exod 9:23-24) is a paradox of simultaneous opposing elements, signaling that normal nature is suspended under divine judgment.
33: wayyak gaphnnam uteenatam — hikah (to strike down) is the same verb later used for the firstborn (v36). The progression of plagues strikes agricultural infrastructure: vines, fig trees, then all trees. wayyshaber etz gevulam (he shattered the trees of their border) — shabar (break/shatter) is the word for breaking idols and crushing enemies; nature itself is shattered by the plague.
34: amar wayyavo arbeh — again the bare amar (he spoke and locusts came). Two locust species: arbeh (mature locust, the common word) and yeleq (young locust, a less-developed stage) — together they cover the full life cycle of the locust swarm, leaving nothing unconsumed.
35: wayyochal kol-esev beartzam — the totality of consumption is emphasized: kol (all) appears twice. esev (vegetation) and peri admatam (fruit of their soil) are the categories of Ps 104's creation abundance — what God provides in creation, the locust plague devours. The land becomes anti-creation.
36: wayyak kol-bchor beartzam — the tenth plague strikes the pinnacle of biological priority. reshit kol-onam (firstfruits of all their vigor) — on (vigor/generative power) appears in Jacob's deathbed poem (Gen 49:3) and Deut 21:17 for the firstborn's special claim. Egypt's firstborn are their generational wealth and social capital — both are struck simultaneously.
37: wayyotziem bkeseph vzahav — the Exodus verb par excellence: yatzah (bring out). Israel departs loaded with silver and gold (Exod 12:35-36), the plundering of Egypt that fulfills God's promise to Abraham (Gen 15:14). wein bshvataw kosher (not one stumbling/weak among their tribes) — after 400 years of slave labor, no one lacks the strength to march. A final miracle in itself.
38: samach mitzrayim btzeetam — the reversal of v24's dynamic: Egypt that once feared Israel's growth now rejoices at their departure. ki-naphal pachadam aleyhem (for the dread of them had fallen upon them) — pachad (sudden terror/dread) is the same word for the awe that falls on surrounding nations during conquest (Gen 35:5; Exod 15:16). Egypt's relief is a backhanded testimony to Israel's God.
39: paras anan lmasach — masach (curtain/screen) is the tabernacle term for the veil (Exod 26:36; 35:12). The cloud as covering uses the same word — wilderness protection is structurally homologous to the tabernacle's inner sanctum. God shelters his people with the same conceptual fabric that shields his glory. wesh lehair layilah — the fire pillar continues the day-night totality of provision.
40: shaaal wayyave slav — shahal (to ask/request) recalls Israel's demanding petition (Num 11:4-6, 31-34). God gives the quail but at terrible cost there (Num 11:33) — the psalmist omits the accompanying judgment, focusing on the provision. lechem shamayim yasviem (bread of heaven satisfied them) — the manna is bread of heaven (lechem shamayim, cf. Exod 16:4; John 6:32-33), a category of provision exceeding earthly agriculture.
41: patach tzur wayyazuvu mayim — patach (to open) applied to a rock makes the stone porous like a spring. The tradition is attested at Rephidim (Exod 17) and Meribah (Num 20); Paul sees one spiritual rock following Israel (1 Cor 10:4). halchu btziyyot nahar (they flowed in dry places like a river) — the desert is transformed into a riverine landscape: barrenness becomes abundance.
42: ki-zachar et-dvar qodsho — the structural keystone: zachar (he remembered) echoes v8 where the same verb established the psalm's premise. The entirety of the Joseph narrative, the plagues, the wilderness provision — all of it flows from a single act: God's covenantal memory. dvar qodsho (his holy word/promise) — the promise to Abraham is holy because it carries God's own character.
43: wayyotzi amow bsasson — sasson (exultation/rejoicing) and rinnah (ringing cry of joy, jubilant shout) are among the most festive Hebrew terms. rinnah denotes the kind of shout that bursts out involuntarily — the Exodus departure is not a solemn retreat but an eruption of joy. amow (his people) and bhirayw (his chosen ones) — election produces belonging, and belonging produces exultation.
44: wayyitten lahem artzot goyim — the land gift is framed in terms of prior labor: waamal leummim yirashu (they inherited the labor of peoples). Israel's inheritance is not vacant land but developed territory — farms, cities, vineyards already planted (Deut 6:10-11). The gift includes the fruits of others' labor, an unmerited grace underscoring that the land is entirely God's to give.
45: baavur yishmru chuqqayw — baavur (so that, in order that) reveals the telos of the entire narrative: all of creation-election-rescue-provision culminates in a people who keep Torah. chuqqayw (his statutes) and toratayw (his instructions) frame covenant obedience as the goal of redemption, not its precondition. The psalm closes with hallelu-Yah — paired with Ps 106's opening and closing doxologies to frame Book IV's great historical meditation.
